moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with an unusual move that begins the Hippopotamus Attack—a solid, patient setup where we build a fortress-like pawn structure before launching any kingside attack. The move a3 prepares to advance the b-pawn and prevents Black from using b4 to harass our pieces later. This isn't a direct assault; instead, we're establishing control over our half of the board and waiting for Black to commit their pieces so we can exploit any weaknesses.

Black seizes space in the center immediately, which is natural, but this allows us to execute our Hippopotamus plan with extra precision. By playing e5, Black has committed their central pawn, and we can now build around it with our methodical pawn chain a3–b3–c3–d3–e3–f3–g3.

We continue our signature setup, advancing the b-pawn and preparing to fianchetto our light-squared bishop on b2, where it will control the long diagonal and support our entire formation. Notice we're playing every move with the same idea: creating a rock-solid base before our pieces even develop. This move also keeps our position flexible and prevents Black from executing an aggressive plan.

Black pushes in the center again, occupying d5 with force. This is actually what we want—Black is spending moves on central play while we're methodically constructing our defensive wall. Their pawns on d5 and e5 are advanced but not yet supported by pieces, which may become a target later.

The third pawn in our chain advances, supporting the d4 square and preparing to eventually bring in our queen and bishop along the long diagonal. The move c3 is crucial because it gives us flexibility to play d3 and e3 without leaving any squares hanging. We're essentially saying to Black: 'Play aggressively if you like, but we have time to build the perfect setup.'

Black develops their kingside knight toward the center, a logical move that pressures e4 and d4 indirectly while preparing to castle kingside. This is solid play from Black, but they're following a conventional development plan while we maintain our unique, positional approach.

Now we move the d-pawn forward, completing the skeleton of our setup and controlling key central squares like e4 and c4. The d3 pawn also prepares to support our kingside when we eventually fianchetto our king's bishop on g2. Each pawn move has been carefully ordered to maintain maximum flexibility.

Black develops the queen's knight, placing it actively on c6 where it eyes d4 and e4 as potential outposts. Their development is clean and purposeful, but we still maintain our patient, territorial approach. Notice that Black is spending moves on piece development while we're completing our pawn structure.

The fourth pawn of our chain slides forward, controlling d4 and f4 while making room for our kingside bishop on f2 or our queen on e2. The e3 pawn is the final central component of our fortress; from here, our position becomes nearly impenetrable. This move also ensures that after we fianchetto both bishops, our king will have a safe home and our minor pieces will dominate.

Black places their light-squared bishop actively on d6, aiming toward our kingside and eyeing h2. This is one of Black's more forcing moves, showing they want to create threats rather than simply develop mechanically. However, our solid pawn chain remains unimpressed by this activity.

We advance the f-pawn to prepare our kingside fianchetto, and importantly, this move prevents Black from playing ...e4, which would be uncomfortable for us. The f3 pawn also supports the e4 square and creates a solid base for our king's safety. Every single pawn on our first four ranks now supports the next rank forward—we're creating an almost impregnable setup.

Black castles kingside, moving their king to safety and connecting their rooks—a prudent decision that completes their development. Their position is solid, but we've now established our complete pawn structure and can begin our piece placement with a huge positional advantage.

The final pawn in our famous chain advances, preparing the ultimate fianchetto of our king's bishop on g2, where it will command the long diagonal and provide devastating support for our entire position. This completes the Hippopotamus setup: a wall of pawns (a3–b3–c3–d3–e3–f3–g3) that controls the center and prepares to channel our pieces into perfectly harmonious squares. Black now faces a fortress they must methodically break down while we prepare to unleash our bishops on the diagonals.
